modern.full: This is a longish 'a' sound, like you would hear in the words 'alms' or 'father.' Note that you will not hear a sound similar to the first 'a' in the word 'animal.' So, the word 'Άλογο' is pronounced 'ah-loh-ghoh.'
Although it looks a lot like a 'b,' the βήτα is pronounced very much like the English 'v,' and in fact, the letter itself is pronounced 'vee-tah.' So, the word 'Βουβάλι' is pronounced 'voo-vah-lee.'

Gamma is a tricky one! Before the vowels α, ο, and ου (and before consonants), it's not a hard 'g,' but neither is it a soft 'h' — it's a soft, gargled 'gh.' Before the vowels ε and ι, it instead sounds like the 'y' in 'yes.' 'Γάτα' is pronounced 'ghah-tah.'
modern.short: a soft, gargled 'gh' (or a 'y' sound before 'e' and 'ee' vowels)

modern.full: Despite looking like an English 'd,' the delta is pronounced like the 'th' sound in the words 'these' and 'those.' (It is not, however, pronounced like the softer 'th' in 'bath' or 'death.') So, 'Διαβάζω' is pronounced 'thee-ah-vah-zoe.'
Epsilon sounds like a short 'e' in English, meaning it's similar in sound to the 'e' in 'held' or 'pen,' but not the longer 'e' as in 'evil' or 'emit.' 'Έχω' is pronounced 'eh-ho.'
Zeta is pronounced like the English 'z.' So, the word 'Ζωή' is pronounced 'zoh-ee.'

alphabetItems.exampleWord: Κρήτη, the name of the island we call 'Crete'

modern.full: Eta is pronounced like the 'ee' in 'feet' or 'greet.' This means that 'Κρήτη' is pronounced 'kree-tee.'
The theta is pronounced like a soft 'th,' as in 'path' or 'mathematics.' 'Θέμα' is pronounced 'theh-mah.'
Iota sounds like a long i (meaning the letter itself is pronounced like 'yota'). It sounds like the 'i' sound in 'machine,' meaning 'κίτρινο' is pronounced 'kee-tree-noh.'
Kappa is identical to the sound an English 'k' makes. So, 'Κορίτσι' is pronounced 'koh-ree-tsee.'
Lambda is pronounced like the English 'l,' which means that the word 'Λέει' is pronounced 'lay-ee.'
Mu is pronounced like the English 'm,' meaning that 'Μαμά' is pronounced 'ma-ma.'
Nu has a pronunciation identical to the English 'n.' 'Πίνουν' is pronounced 'pee-noon.'
Xi is pronounced like the English letter 'x,' although it can be used at any point in the word, including the beginning, without changing its pronunciation. So, 'Ξένος' is pronounced 'kseh-nos.'

In modern Greek, omicron and omega make exactly the same 'o' sound, as in 'over' — only the spelling differs. A handy pattern: when a word ends in an omicron, it tends to be a noun, adjective, or article, but not a verb. 'Όνομα' is pronounced 'OH-no-mah,' with the stress on the accented first syllable.
modern.short: an 'o,' as in 'over' — ends many adjectives, articles, and nouns

modern.full: Pi is pronounced like the English 'p' (and the letter itself is pronounced like 'pee'), which means that 'Πόσο' is pronounced 'po-so.'

Rho's pronunciation is similar to the English 'r,' but it is slightly trilled, similar to how an 'r' is pronounced in Spanish or Italian. Make sure your tongue hits the roof of your mouth when you say the rho in 'ρόδα,' which is pronounced '´roh-tha.'
Sigma is pronounced like the English 's.' So, the word 'σκούρος,' which means 'dark,' is pronounced with the same 's' sound as its English counterpart. Say it 'skoo-ros.'
Tau is pronounced like an English 't.' The word 'Τι' is pronounced as it's spelled: 'ti.'

Ypsilon is an 'ee' sound, like the vowel version of 'y' — for example, the 'y' sound in 'candy.' The accent simply marks the stressed syllable, so 'Ύμνος' is pronounced 'EEM-nos.' (The 'h' in the English word 'hymn' comes from an ancient Greek 'rough breathing' mark that is no longer pronounced in modern Greek.)
modern.short: a long 'ee' sound like the vowel 'y' in 'candy'

modern.full: Phi is pronounced like the English 'f.' 'Ελέφαντας' is pronounced 'eh-leh-fan-tas.'

Chi (in Greek, the letter is pronounced like 'hee') is a breathy 'h' sound — harder than an English 'h,' like the 'ch' in the Scottish 'loch' or German 'Bach,' and softer (like the 'h' in 'hue') before 'e' and 'ee' sounds. So, the word 'όχι' sounds like 'oh-hee.'

True to its name, the letter Ψ is pronounced as a combination of the consonant sounds of 'p' and 's,' and it can be found in any place in the word, including the very beginning. It sounds the same as the 'ps' combination in the English words 'claps' or 'traps,' so 'Ψυχή' is pronounced 'psee-hee.'
modern.short: a combination of the consonant sounds 'p' and 's,' forming a 'ps' as in 'cyclops'

modern.full: Omega is an 'o' sound, as in 'over' or 'post.' In modern Greek it is pronounced exactly the same as the omicron — the difference is spelling and grammar: when a word ends in an omega, that word tends to be a verb. The word 'Ωκεανός' is pronounced 'oh-keh-ah-nos.'
modern.short: an 'o,' as in 'over' — ends many verbs

modern.full: This is a longish 'a' sound, like you would hear in the words 'alms' or 'father.' Note that you will not hear a sound similar to the first 'a' in the word 'animal.' The word 'γράμμα' is pronounced 'ghrah-mah.'

Although it looks a lot like a 'b,' the βήτα is pronounced very much like the English 'v,' and in fact, the letter itself is pronounced 'vee-tah.' Therefore, the word 'βιβλίο' is pronounced 'vee-vlee-oh.'

Gamma is a tricky one! Before the vowels α, ο, and ου (and before consonants), it's a soft, gargled 'gh' — not a hard 'g,' but not a soft 'h' either. Before the vowels ε and ι, it sounds like the 'y' in 'yes.' That's why 'βιολογία' is pronounced 'vee-oh-lo-yee-ah.'
modern.short: a soft, gargled 'gh' (or a 'y' sound before 'e' and 'ee' vowels)

modern.full: Despite looking like an English 'd,' the delta is pronounced like the 'th' sound in the words 'these' and 'those.' (It is not, however, pronounced like the softer 'th' in 'bath' or 'death.') So, 'διαμάντι' is pronounced 'thee-ah-mahn-tee.'
Epsilon sounds like a short 'e' in English, meaning it's similar in sound to the 'e' in 'held' or 'pen,' but not the longer 'e' as in 'evil' or 'emit.' 'Μέση' is pronounced 'meh-see.'
Zeta is pronounced like the English 'z.' So, the word 'ζουζούνι' is pronounced like 'zoo-zoo-nee.'
Eta is pronounced like the 'ee' in 'feet' or 'greet.' This means that 'αράχνη' is pronounced 'ah-rah-hnee.'
The theta is pronounced like a soft 'th,' as in 'path' or 'mathematics' (not a coincidence), meaning that 'μαθηματικά' is pronounced, as you would expect, 'mah-thee-mah-tee-kah.'
Iota sounds like a long i (meaning the letter itself is pronounced like 'yota'). It sounds like the 'i' sound in 'machine,' meaning 'κόκκινη' is pronounced 'koh-ki-nee.'
Kappa is identical to the sound an English 'k' makes. So, 'πορτοκαλί' is pronounced 'por-toe-kah-lee.'
Lambda is pronounced like the English 'l,' which means that the word 'καλό' is pronounced 'kah-low.'
Mu is pronounced like the English 'm,' meaning that 'ομόνοια' is pronounced 'oh-moe-nee-ah'
Nu has a pronunciation identical to the English 'n.' 'Νερό' is pronounced 'neh-roe.'
Xi is pronounced like the English letter 'x,' and it can be used at any point in the word, including the beginning, without changing its pronunciation. 'Λεξικό' is pronounced 'leks-ih-ko.'

In modern Greek, omicron and omega make exactly the same 'o' sound, as in 'over' — only the spelling differs. A handy pattern: when a word ends in an omicron, it tends to be a noun, adjective, or article, but not a verb. Because the omicron ends the word 'φρέσκο,' it is pronounced 'fres-koh.'
modern.short: an 'o,' as in 'over' — ends many adjectives, articles, and nouns

modern.full: Pi is pronounced like the English 'p' (and the letter itself is pronounced like 'pee'), which means 'πάπια' is pronounced 'pah-pee-ah.'

Rho's pronunciation is similar to the English 'r,' but it is slightly trilled, similar to how an 'r' is pronounced in Spanish or Italian. Make sure your tongue hits the roof of your mouth when you reach the rho in 'παρακαλώ,' which is pronounced 'pah-´rah-kah-lo.'
Sigma is pronounced like the English 's.' So, the word 'στάση,' which means 'stop,' is pronounced with the same 's' sound as its English counterpart. Say it 'stah-see.'
Sigma is pronounced like the English 's.' So, the word 'γάτες,' which means 'cats,' is pronounced with the same 's' sound as its English counterpart. Say it 'ghah-tes.'
Tau is pronounced like an English 't.' The word 'τρώει' is pronounced 'troh-ee.'
Ypsilon is a 'ee' sound, or a long 'i' as in 'machine.' So, 'σκύλος' is pronounced like 'skee-los.'
modern.short: a long 'ee' sound like the vowel 'y' in 'candy'

modern.full: Phi is pronounced like the English 'f.' 'Καφές' is pronounced 'kah-fes.'

Chi (in Greek, the letter is pronounced like 'hee') is a breathy 'h' sound — harder than an English 'h,' like the 'ch' in the Scottish 'loch' or German 'Bach,' and softer (like the 'h' in 'hue') before 'e' and 'ee' sounds. 'Χυμός' is pronounced 'hee-MOS.'

The letter Ψ is pronounced as a combination of the consonant sounds of 'p' and 's,' and it can be found in any place in the word, including the very beginning. It sounds the same as the 'ps' combination in the English words 'claps' or 'traps,' so 'ψάρι' is pronounced 'psah-ree.'
modern.short: a combination of the consonant sounds 'p' and 's,' forming a 'ps' as in 'cyclops'

modern.full: Omega is an 'o' sound, as in 'over' or 'post.' In modern Greek it is pronounced exactly the same as the omicron — the difference is spelling and grammar: when a word ends in an omega, that word tends to be a verb. The word 'πρώτα' is pronounced 'pro-tah.'
modern.short: an 'o,' as in 'over' — ends many verbs

modern.full: This combination has a short-e sound, like 'eh.' So, 'παιδί' sounds like 'peh-thee.'

alphabetItems.explanation: This is a combination of the vowels alpha (α) and iota (ι).

modern.full: This combination usually has an 'ahf' sound, with a distinct 'f,' when it comes before a voiceless consonant — so 'αυτό' sounds like 'af-to.' Before a vowel or a voiced consonant it sounds like 'av' instead: 'αύριο,' which means 'tomorrow,' is pronounced 'AV-ree-oh.'

alphabetItems.explanation: This is a combination of the vowels alpha (α) and ypsilon (υ).

modern.full: This combination makes a sound like the 'ee' in 'feet' or 'machine,' meaning 'εκείνοι' is pronounced 'eh-kee-nee.'

alphabetItems.explanation: This is a combination of the vowels epsilon (ε) and iota (ι).

modern.full: This combination usually has an 'eff' sound, with a distinct 'f' despite not containing a φ, when it comes before a voiceless consonant — so 'ευχαριστώ' sounds like 'eff-har-ee-sto.' Before a vowel or a voiced consonant it sounds like 'ev' instead: 'Ευρώπη,' which means 'Europe,' is pronounced 'ev-RO-pee.'

alphabetItems.explanation: This is a combination of the vowels epsilon (ε) and ypsilon (υ).

modern.full: This combination makes a sound like 'ee.' 'Όλοι' is pronounced 'oh-lee.'

alphabetItems.explanation: This is a combination of the vowels omicron (ο) and iota (ι).

modern.full: This combination sounds like a long 'oo,' like the English words 'school' or 'pool.' 'Σκούρο' is pronounced 'skoo-ro.'

alphabetItems.explanation: This is a combination of the vowels omicron (ο) and ypsilon (υ).

modern.full: This combination sounds like an English 'ng' as in 'sing' or the word 'English' itself. So, 'Αγγλικά' is pronounced 'ang-lee-ka.'

alphabetItems.explanation: This is a combination of two gammas (γγ), but it has a sound different from a single gamma.

modern.full: At the start of a word, this combination sounds like a hard English 'g,' as in 'go' — so 'γκολ' is pronounced 'gohl.' In the middle of a word it often picks up a nasal sound, like the 'ng' in 'finger': 'αγκαλιά,' which means 'hug,' is pronounced 'ang-gah-lyah.'

alphabetItems.explanation: This is a combination of the consonants gamma and kappa, but it sounds unlike either one alone.

modern.full: This combination adds a soft 'ng' sound with a breathy 'gh' sound like that of the χ itself. So 'άγχος' is pronounced 'ang-hohs.'

alphabetItems.explanation: This is a combination of the consonants gamma and chi, but although the chi sound shines through at the end, the combination creates an 'ng' sound as well.

modern.full: While the beta would seem like a good candidate to have a 'b' sound, it really acts as a 'v,' leaving this consonant cluster to sound like the English 'b.' Thus, μπαμπάς sounds like 'ba-bas.'

alphabetItems.explanation: This is a combination of the consonants mu and pi, but sounds unlike either one alone.

modern.full: This combination sounds very much like an English 'd.' Given that the delta is more like a hard 'th,' this consonant cluster takes the place of the 'd' sound. 'Αντίο' is pronounced very much like the French 'adieu,' like 'ah-dee-oh.'

alphabetItems.explanation: This is a combination of the consonants nu and tau, but sounds unlike either one alone.

modern.full: This combination sounds like 'ts,' as in the English word 'cats.' 'Τσάι' is pronounced 'tsah-ee.'

alphabetItems.explanation: This is a combination of the consonants tau and sigma, but it sounds unlike either one alone.

modern.full: This combination sounds like 'dz,' a bit like the 'ds' in 'lads.' So, 'τζατζίκι' is pronounced 'dzah-dzee-kee.'

alphabetItems.explanation: This is a combination of the consonants tau and zeta, but it sounds unlike either one alone.
